title-group.article-title: Less frequent intravenous dosing of nemvaleukin alfa in patients with advanced solid tumors: the phase 1/2 ARTISTRY-3 trial

contrib.aff: Department of Investigational Cancer Therapeutics, Division of Cancer Medicine, The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, Houston, TX 77030, United States
START Mountain Region, West Valley City, UT 84119, United States

UTEC Hospital Clínico San Carlos, Madrid 28040, Spain

Mural Oncology, Waltham, MA 02451, United States

author-notes.corresp: Corresponding author: Sarina A. Piha-Paul, Department of Investigational Cancer Therapeutics, Division of Cancer Medicine, The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, Houston, TX 77030, USA (spihapau@mdanderson.org).

fn.p: Principal Investigator: Sarina A. Piha-Paul

permissions.copyright-statement: © The Author(s) 2025. Published by Oxford University Press.

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits non-commercial re-use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. For commercial re-use, please contact reprints@oup.com for reprints and translation rights for reprints. All other permissions can be obtained through our RightsLink service via the Permissions link on the article page on our site—for further information please contact journals.permissions@oup.com .

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Intravenous (IV) nemvaleukin alfa (nemvaleukin, ALKS 4230) administered daily on days 1-5 in 21‑day cycles demonstrated antitumor activity and manageable safety in heavily pretreated advanced solid tumors. We present results from cohort 2 of the open-label phase I/II ARTISTRY-3 (NCT04592653) study, which evaluated less frequent IV dosing of nemvaleukin in advanced solid tumors.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Eligible patients received escalating IV nemvaleukin doses in 21-day cycles on 3 schedules: day 1, days 1 and 8, and days 1 and 4. The primary endpoint was the incidence of dose-limiting toxicities (DLTs).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: From April 2022 to June 2024, 52 patients received nemvaleukin. No DLTs were reported. Most treatment-related adverse events (TRAEs) were grade 1-2. Six patients (12%) experienced grade 3 TRAEs, the most common being neutropenia. Nemvaleukin exposure increased with escalating doses. Natural killer (NK) cell and CD8+ T-cell expansion in whole blood was observed, with minimal regulatory T-cell expansion. Nemvaleukin at 30 μg/kg on days 1 and 8 was the recommended phase II dose. No objective responses were observed; 16 (31%) patients had stable disease (6 [12%] for ≥3 months). Increased tumor microenvironment infiltration of NK cells and CD8+ T-cells was observed in on-treatment biopsies.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Less frequent IV doses of nemvaleukin demonstrated pharmacodynamic proof of mechanism and were tolerable with some disease stabilization.

list-item.p: The phase I/II ARTISTRY-3 study identified a less-frequent nemvaleukin dosing schedule (30 μg/kg on days 1 and 8) that was patient- and provider-friendly compared with the more frequent 5 consecutive daily regimen.
Nemvaleukin demonstrated manageable safety and pharmacodynamic proof of mechanism with some disease stabilization, comparable to the effects observed with the 5 consecutive daily infusion regimen.
Natural killer (NK) cell and CD8+ T-cell expansion was observed in whole blood, along with increased tumor microenvironment infiltration, with minimal regulatory T-cell expansion.

tr.td: At least 1 approved indication-specific prior therapy
Incidence of dose-limiting toxicities (DLTs) from the first dose through the end of the DLT observation period

Safety, overall response rate (ORR), and duration of response based on Response Evaluation Criteria In Solid Tumors v1.1, serum concentrations of nemvaleukin and descriptive pharmacokinetic parameters, and changes in numbers of circulating immune cells in peripheral blood in pre-treatment and on-treatment samples

sec.title: Additional details of endpoints or study design
Study design and treatments
sec.p: ARTISTRY-3 (NCT04592653, Figure 1) is a phase I/II multicenter, open-label, sequential-group, adaptive-design study of intravenous (IV) nemvaleukin in patients with advanced solid tumors. The study enrolled patients at 6 sites, 4 in the United States and 2 in Spain. Bayesian optimal interval design was followed for dose escalation, with modifications to accommodate open enrollment. Dose escalation was based on predefined safety parameters of DLT evaluated during the first cycle (21 days) by the safety review committee. Escalating doses of nemvaleukin were administered via a 30-minute IV infusion in 21-day cycles across 3 schedules as follows: Schedule 1, nemvaleukin on day 1 at 10, 20, 30, 35, and 40 μg/kg; Schedule 2, nemvaleukin on days 1 and 8 at 20, 25 and 30 μg/kg; Schedule 3, nemvaleukin on days 1 and 4 at 15, 20, 25, 30 and 35 μg/kg. Schedules 2 and 3 were opened after the maximum tolerated dose was determined and all expected dose levels were considered tolerable for Schedule 1 in consultation with the safety review committee. For all schedules, additional higher dose levels could continue in increments of 5 μg/kg from the highest dose if tolerable and pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic parameters were not saturated for at least 1 to 2 dose level. The study protocol and all amendments were approved by the institutional review board or independent ethics committee at each site. A safety review committee monitored safety and supported the dose escalation decision and recommended phase II dose (RP2D) selection. All participants provided written informed consent according to the principles of the Declaration of Helsinki.

caption.p: Characterization of responses. (A) Swimmer plot of duration of treatment and response evaluation. Each bar represents 1 patient from the first dose until the end of treatment. (B) Waterfall plot of the best percentage change from baseline in target lesions. Best change from baseline (%) expressed as percent change from baseline at maximum reduction in sum of target lesion diameters until first PD or new anticancer therapy. Patients without postbaseline tumor assessment are not shown. Horizontal reference line indicates the 30% reduction consistent with a response as per RECIST v1.1. PD, progressive disease; SD, stable disease.

sec.title: Study population
sec.p: Eligible patients were aged ≥18 years and had a measurable disease based on Response Evaluation Criteria In Solid Tumors (RECIST) v1.1 and an Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status of 0 or 1. Patients had histologically or cytologically confirmed solid tumors, had exhausted all lines of treatment, and had progressed or developed intolerance to them.

sec.title: Study objectives and endpoints
sec.p: The primary objectives were to investigate the safety and tolerability of less frequent IV dosing schedules of nemvaleukin, to identify an RP2D, and to determine the maximum tolerated dose of nemvaleukin as monotherapy. Secondary objectives were to evaluate clinical antitumor activity, safety, pharmacodynamics, pharmacokinetics, and immunogenicity of nemvaleukin. The primary endpoint was the incidence of DLTs from the first dose through the end of the DLT observation period. Secondary endpoints included incidence and severity of adverse events (AEs), overall response rate (ORR), and duration of response based on RECIST v1.1, serum concentrations of nemvaleukin and descriptive pharmacokinetic parameters, and changes in numbers of circulating immune cells in peripheral blood in pre-treatment and on-treatment samples.

sec.title: Study assessments
sec.p: Safety was evaluated based on AEs, vital signs and weight, clinical laboratory tests, and standard 12-lead electrocardiograms. Treatment-emergent AEs were defined as AEs that occurred or worsened after the first dose of study drug. Reported AE terms were coded using the Medical Dictionary for Regulatory Activities (MedDRA) v27.0.

ORR and duration of response were evaluated based on investigator’s assessment. All target and nontarget lesions were measured radiographically or photographically for superficial skin tumors. ORR was calculated as the proportion of patients with confirmed complete response (CR) or partial response (PR) per RECIST v1.1. Whole blood samples were collected at the specified time points and processed using flow cytometry for pharmacodynamic assessment of circulating immune cells and their subtypes.

Fresh tumor biopsies were collected pre-treatment on screening day and on-treatment during cycle 2 day 8 through day 12. Deep immune profiling using a 14-marker panel encompassing various activation states of natural killer (NK), CD8+ T-, CD4+ T-, and regulatory T (Treg) cells, plasma cells, macrophages, and tumor markers was performed. Multiplex immunofluorescence (mIF) staining was performed on 4-µm-thick formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded sections of tumor biopsy tissue to evaluate expression of phenotypic and functional immune cell markers on tumor-infiltrating leukocytes using the MultiOmyxTM platform (NeoGenomics). The expression of 14 markers (cluster of differentiation 3 [CD3], program cell death protein 1 [PD-1], CD4, CD163, CD8, granzyme B, forkhead box protein P3 [FOXP3], CD45RO, CD38, nature killer p46 [NKp46], CD138, T cell factor 1/7 [TCF-1/TCF-7], and pan cytokeratin [PanCK] or Sry-related HMg-Box gene 10 [SOX10]) was investigated for cell quantification and spatial analytics. Noncompartmental pharmacokinetic analysis was performed to estimate the pharmacokinetic parameters for nemvaleukin. Concentrations of nemvaleukin were quantified in serum samples using a validated electrochemiluminescence method.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: The maximum sample size was 30 patients per schedule, and up to 9 patients were enrolled in a particular dosing level. DLTs were analyzed based on the DLT-evaluable population, which included patients who had received the specified dose of nemvaleukin during the first cycle as per the schedule assigned and had completed 21-day follow-up in cycle 1 or had a DLT during cycle 1. ORR and safety were analyzed based on the safety population, which included all patients who had received at least 1 dose of nemvaleukin. The pharmacokinetic population included patients who received at least 1 dose of nemvaleukin and had at least 1 measurable serum concentration of nemvaleukin at any scheduled pharmacokinetic time point. The peripheral blood pharmacodynamics population included patients who received at least 1 dose of nemvaleukin and had both baseline and at least 1 postbaseline available peripheral blood pharmacodynamic measurement. ORR was summarized by frequency counts, percentage, and 95% confidence interval (CI). In general, summary statistics (n, mean, standard deviation, median, minimum, and maximum for continuous variables, and number [%] of patients in each category for categorical variables) were provided for all variables. The data were summarized by dosing schedule and dose levels.

tr.td: Ovarian cancer (n=2); pancreatic cancer (n=3); mucosal melanoma (n=2); cutaneous melanoma (n=2); epithelial tumor of the fallopian tube, peritoneum, or ovaries (n=3); othersa (n=5).

Ovarian cancer (n=3); pancreatic cancer (n=2); cutaneous melanoma (n=1); biliary tract tumorb (n=2); cervical cancer (n=2); epithelial tumor of the fallopian tube, peritoneum, or ovaries (n=1); othersa (n=2)
Ovarian cancer (n=3); pancreatic cancer (n=2); mucosal melanoma (n=2); cutaneous melanoma (n=1); biliary tract tumorb (n=2); cervical cancer (n=2) ; othersa (n=10)

Ovarian cancer (n=8); pancreatic cancer, (n=7); mucosal melanoma (n=4); cutaneous melanoma (n=4); biliary tract tumorb (n=4); cervical cancer (n=4); epithelial tumor of the fallopian tube, peritoneum, or ovaries (n=4); othersa (n=17)

fn.label: a
fn.p: Gastric and gastroesophageal junction adenocarcinoma (1 patient each in Schedule 1, Schedule 2, and Schedule 3), endometrial cancer (2 patients in Schedule 1), metastatic or advanced breast cancer (2 patients in Schedule 3), esophageal cancer (squamous and adeno cell type; 1 patient each in Schedule 1 and Schedule 3), vulvar melanoma (2 patients in Schedule 3), adenocarcinoma of the left ovary (1 patient in Schedule 3), borderline mucinous ovarian carcinoma (1 patient in Schedule 2), high-grade serous adenocarcinoma of the ovary (1 patient in Schedule 3), metastatic multifocal vulvar melanoma (1 patient in Schedule 3), nodular melanoma (Breslow) (1 patient in Schedule 3), triple-negative breast cancer (1 patient in Schedule 1).

fn.label: b
fn.p: Including intra- and extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, gall bladder, ampullary type.
Additional patient characteristics and the patient enrollment schema are shown in Table 1 and Figure 1, respectively.
Abbreviation: ECOG, Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group.

tr.td: ORR, calculated as the proportion of patients with confirmed complete response (CR) or partial response (PR)
52 were treated in Schedules 1, 2, and 3 (17, 13, and 22 patients, respectively)
Number of patients evaluable for toxicity
Number of patients evaluated for efficacy

fn.p: See Figure 2A and B for the swimmer plot of duration of treatment and response evaluation, and the waterfall plot of the best percentage change from baseline in target lesions, respectively.
Abbreviations: CI, confidence interval; ORR, overall response rate; PD, progressive disease; RECIST, Response Evaluation Criteria In Solid Tumors; SD, stable disease.

tr.td: Any grade nemvaleukin-related TEAEs (≥20% in at least 1 schedule), n (%)

fn.label: a
fn.p: In Schedule 1: The median total number of cycles was 2 (range, 1-8); median duration of exposure was 6 weeks (range, 3-25); median relative dose intensity was 100% (range, 98-106), and median total dose received was 60 µg/kg (range, 10-239). Nemvaleukin dose interruption/delay due to any AE occurred in 4 patients (24%), and there were no nemvaleukin dose reductions due to any AEs.

fn.label: b
fn.p: Patients treated with any doses in cycle X will be counted as having received X cycles. Patient will be counted to the maximum number of cycles treated. Duration of exposure (weeks) = (last dose date—first dose date +d + 1)/7, where d is 0 if the patient discontinued treatment during the cycle; otherwise, d is 20, 13, and 17 for Schedules 1, 2, and 3, respectively. Relative dose intensity (%) = 100 × [total dose received/total protocol planned dose].

fn.label: c
fn.p: In Schedule 2: The median total number of cycles was 2 (range, 1-9); median duration of exposure was 6 weeks (range, 0.1-27); median relative dose intensity was 100% (range, 50-102), and median total dose received was 100 µg/kg (range, 25-423). Nemvaleukin dose interruption/delay due to any AE occurred in 4 patients (31%), and there were no nemvaleukin dose reductions due to any AEs.

fn.label: d
fn.p: In Schedule 3: The median total number of cycles was 3 (range, 1-13); median duration of exposure was 8 weeks (range, 0.1-39); median relative dose intensity was 100% (range, 49-104), and median total dose received was 140 µg/kg (range, 34-781). Nemvaleukin dose interruption/delay due to any AE occurred in 7 patients (32%), and there were 3 patients (14%) who had nemvaleukin dose reductions due to any AEs.

fn.label: e
fn.p: One patient in nemvaleukin 20 µg/kg dose group.

fn.label: f
fn.p: One patient in nemvaleukin 25 µg/kg dose group.

fn.label: g
fn.p: One patient each in nemvaleukin 25, 30, and 35 µg/kg dose groups.

fn.label: h
fn.p: One patient in nemvaleukin 35 µg/kg dose group.

fn.label: i
fn.p: One patient in nemvaleukin 30 µg/kg dose group.
Abbreviations: AE, adverse event; TEAE, treatment-emergent adverse event; TRAE, treatment-related adverse event.

tr.th: Pharmacokinetics/pharmacodynamics: nemvaleukin pharmacokinetic parameters at cycle 1 day 1

fn.label: a
fn.p: Values are median. The entire concentration-time profile and estimation of the terminal log-linear decline phase (λz) could not be optimally captured. Therefore, subsequently planned pharmacokinetic parameters that require λz could not be computed.

Abbreviations: AUClast, area under the concentration-time curve from time zero to last quantifiable concentration; Clast, last observed quantifiable concentration; Cmax, maximum observed concentration; n/a, not available; SD, standard deviation; Tlast, time of last measurable (positive) observed concentration; Tmax, time to Cmax.

sec.title: Pharmacokinetics
sec.p: Nemvaleukin exposure (maximum observed concentration [Cmax]) and area under the concentration-time curve from time zero to last quantifiable concentration (AUClast) increased proportionally with increasing dose for each schedule, with no observed evidence of nonlinearity at the highest dose evaluated (Figure 3). Exposures after repeated administration were similar to those at the first dose, and accumulation was not observed.

caption.p: Nemvaleukin exposure–dose relationship at cycle 1 day 1. AUClast, area under the concentration-time curve from time zero to last quantifiable C, cycle; Cmax, maximum observed concentration; D, day.

sec.title: Pharmacodynamics from whole blood samples
sec.p: Expansion of NK cells and CD8+ T-cells from whole blood was observed at all dosing schedules of nemvaleukin, with low-level, transient, non-dose-dependent expansion of Tregs (Figure 4A and B). In cycles 1-3, peak levels of NK cells and CD8+ T-cells were observed on day 8 (Figure 4A). The highest maximum fold change in absolute counts (range) of NK cells and CD8+ T-cells from baseline was observed to be 10.4-fold (2.5-10.4) and 4.3-fold (2.0-4.3), respectively, in the 30 μg/kg group in Schedule 2, and 89.1-fold (3.1-89.1) and 19.4-fold (1.9-19.4), respectively, in the 35 μg/kg group in Schedule 3 (Figure 4B). After comprehensive review of the safety, pharmacokinetic, and pharmacodynamic parameters across all schedules and doses, nemvaleukin 30 μg/kg at days 1 and 8 was declared the RP2D.

caption.p: Pharmacodynamic effects of nemvaleukin in whole blood. (A) Immune cell changes over 3 cycles of nemvaleukin treatment. (B) Dose-dependent maximum fold change in circulating NK cells and CD8+ T-cells with maximum time point beyond cycle 1. Gold and blue dotted lines indicate maximum fold change in levels of NK cells and CD8+ T-cells, respectively, observed with nemvaleukin IV QD × 5 dosing in ARTISTRY-1. BL, baseline; C, cycle; D, day; Fmax, maximum fold change from baseline (beyond cycle 1); IV, intravenous; NK, natural killer QD × 5, once daily for 5 consecutive days Treg, regulatory T-cell.

sec.title: Pharmacodynamics from peripheral blood mononuclear cells
sec.p: Further analyses of proliferation and activation states of lymphocyte subsets were conducted in cytopreserved peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) derived from patients in Schedule 2. Robust proliferation of NK cells (CD56bright and CD56dim) in response to nemvaleukin, with nearly all cells exhibiting Ki67 positivity, was observed (data not shown). The maximum Ki67+ values were nearly 100% in all NK cell subsets across doses, indicating that NK cells have a relatively high level of proliferation at baseline. Approximately 50%-80% of PD1+, CD122+, and inducible costimulator (ICOS+)effector memory and terminal effector memory CD8+ T-cells were proliferative, while naive and central memory subsets had lower proliferative proportions (approximately 10%-30%). Overall, CD4+ T-cell subsets proliferated less in response to nemvaleukin with the exception of the CD122+ subset, in which 60%-80% of cells were proliferative. These results were expected since nemvaleukin binds and signals through CD122 (data not shown).

At 25 and 30 µg/kg doses from Schedule 2, the highest induced proliferation (Fmax) occurred in the CD8+ T-cells. Notably, nemvaleukin stimulated proliferation in both naive and effector subsets, demonstrating potential to broadly stimulate the CD8+ T-cells. Induced proliferation was lower in NK cell subsets than in CD8+ T-cell subsets (Figure 5). Induced proliferation was lower in CD4+ T-cell and Treg subsets compared with the CD8+ T-cell and NK cell compartments (Figure 5).

caption.p: Maximum fold change (Fmax) of proliferating immune cell subtypes. CD, cluster of differentiation; CM, central memory; CXCR, C-X-C motif chemokine receptor; EM, effector memory; EMRA, terminally differentiated effector memory cells re-expressing CD45RA; FOXP3, forhead box protein P3; HLADR, human leukocyte antigen-DR isotype; ICOS, inducible costimulator; NK, natural killer; PD1, programmed cell death protein 1; Tregs, regulatory T-cells.

sec.title: Tumor microenvironment analysis
sec.p: Paired tumor biopsy data were available for 8 patients with the following tumor types: ovarian cancer (n = 3), mucosal melanoma (n = 3), cutaneous melanoma (n = 1), and breast cancer (n = 1). Patients had received 3-14 prior lines of therapy. Paired pre-treatment and on-treatment tumor biopsies were subjected to deep immune profiling using mIF staining. Baseline infiltration of NK cells across tumor types was low (<100 cells/mm2). Tumor-associated macrophages were the most abundant cell type across all tumors analyzed. Melanomas were generally more T-cell inflamed than other tumor types, with the exception of 1 case of ovarian cancer (Ovarian cancer #2) (Figure 6A). Increases in NK cell and CD8+ T-cell subsets, especially granzyme B+ (GZB+) subsets, were observed during nemvaleukin treatment across all tumor types (Figure 6B). Increases in CD4+ T-cell subsets and plasma cells were observed in cutaneous melanoma, ovarian, and breast tumors. On-treatment Treg densities varied across tumor types. Little to no increase was observed in patients with mucosal melanoma, but increased densities were noted in cutaneous melanoma, ovarian, and breast tumors (Figure 6B). Nonetheless, nemvaleukin treatment preferentially increased cytolytic effector NK cells and CD8+ T-cells compared with Tregs as evidenced by favorable ratios of both NK and CD8+ effector cells to Tregs on treatment (Figure 6C). These increases were consistent in patients with mucosal melanoma but variable in patients with ovarian cancer, with notable increase in effector cell to Treg ratio observed in Ovarian cancer #1 and Ovarian cancer #2 (Figure 6C). Tumor proximity analysis revealed increased proportions of GZB+ NK cells and GZB+ CD8+ T-cells within tumor cell neighborhoods (Figure 7A), collectively indicating recruitment of cytolytic effectors to the tumor microenvironment (TME). Representative mIF images for paired tumor biopsies for a patient with vulvar mucosal melanoma and a patient with platinum-resistant ovarian cancer after nemvaleukin treatment are depicted in Figure 7B and C. For the patient with vulvar mucosal melanoma, the pre-treatment biopsy was infiltrated with an immune population rich in Treg and CD4+ T-cells, with few NK cells and CD8+ T-cells. Upon nemvaleukin treatment (Schedule 3, 15 µg/kg dosing group), an induction of GZB+ NK cells and CD8+ T-cells was observed in the on-treatment tumor biopsy 4 days post−dose (Figure 7B). Similarly, for the patient with platinum-resistant ovarian cancer, the pre-treatment biopsy was not immune-infiltrated and exhibited a low density of lymphocytes whereas induction of GZB+ effector-rich lymphoid aggregates was observed in the on-treatment biopsy (3 days post-dose, Schedule 3, 15 µg/kg dosing group [Figure 7C]). Of note, this patient exhibited disease stabilization, with a 20% decrease in target lesion, stable disease for ≥3 months, and the highest, as well as the most diverse, immune activation (Figure 6B). Patient with Ovarian cancer #2 exhibited a best overall response of stable disease for >3 months and had an immune infiltrate present at screening.

caption.p: Immune cell densities in paired pre-treatment and on-treatment tumor biopsies. (A) Patient-level immune cell densities in pre-treatment tumor biopsies. (B) Heat map, patient-level fold changes in immune populations; box-and-whisker plot, median densities of effector CD8+ T-cells, NK cells, and Treg. The box represents the interquartile range, the line denotes the median, and the whiskers represent the minimum/maximum values. (C) Patient-level effector immune cell to Treg ratios in pre-treatment and on-treatment biopsies. The dashed line represents ratio of 1.0. CD, cluster of differentiation; GZB, granzyme B; NK, natural killer; PD1, programmed cell death protein 1; TAM, tumor-associated macrophage; TCF, T-cell factor; Treg, regulatory T-cell.

Pharmacodynamic effect of nemvaleukin on tumor microenvironment in paired pre-treatment and on-treatment tumor biopsies. (A) Patient-level proximity analysis for GZB+ NK cells and GZB+ CD8+ T-cells in 20 µm radial tumor cell neighborhoods. (B) Representative H&E and mIF images of tumor biopsies in a patient with vulvar mucosal melanoma. (C) Representative H&E and mIF images of tumor biopsies in a patient with platinum-resistant ovarian cancer. Magnification is 0.4× and 1.58×. White arrows denote Treg in pre-treatment biopsies and GZB+ CD8 T-cells and GZB+ NK cells in on-treatment biopsies.
aPatient with Mucosal melanoma #1 was on dosing Schedule 1 (30 µg/kg) and received 2 treatment cycles.
bPatient with Mucosal melanoma, vulvar #2 was on dosing Schedule 3 (15 µg/kg) and received 2 treatment cycles.
cPatient with Mucosal melanoma #3 was on dosing Schedule 3 (25 µg/kg) and received 2 treatment cycles.
dPatient with Cutaneous melanoma was on dosing Schedule 1 (30 µg/kg) and received 2 treatment cycles.
ePatient with Ovarian cancer #1 was on dosing Schedule 2 (25 µg/kg) and received 9 treatment cycles.
fPatient with Ovarian cancer #2 was on dosing Schedule 1 (30 µg/kg) and received 8 treatment cycles.
gPatient with Ovarian cancer #3 was on dosing Schedule 3 (15 µg/kg) and received 2 treatment cycles.
hPatient with Breast cancer was on dosing Schedule 3 (35 µg/kg) and received 2 treatment cycles.
*No target immune cells noted in the tumor cell neighborhoods.
GZB, granzyme B; H&E, hematoxylin and eosin; mIF, multiplex immunofluorescence; NK, natural killer.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: While immunotherapies, particularly immune checkpoint inhibitors, provide improved outcomes for many patients with advanced solid tumors,1–5 a significant subset of patients remain unresponsive or develop resistance to immune checkpoint inhibitors or encounter tolerability challenges.6–8

The interleukin-2 (IL-2) pathway is a clinically validated immuno-oncology treatment target that highlights the dual action of IL-2: immunosuppressive expansion of Tregs at low doses and antitumor immunostimulatory activation of effector cells, including CD8+ T-cells and NK cells at high doses.9,10 For adults with stage IV solid tumors, IL-2 therapy has historically demonstrated durable remission.11–15 However, recombinant human IL-2 (rhIL-2) administration is limited by its in-patient treatment setting as short bolus infusions every 8 hours over the course of 5 days in 21-day cycles. Furthermore, high doses of rhIL-2 required to achieve clinical efficacy are associated with severe and life-threatening acute toxicities such as capillary leak syndrome, thereby restricting its use to medically fit patients.12,16,17 IL-2 variants with more convenient administration schedules and improved safety profiles are needed to reduce the treatment burden. Nemvaleukin alfa (nemvaleukin, ALKS 4230) is a novel engineered cytokine that is designed to leverage antitumor effects of the IL-2 pathway while limiting typical IL-2–associated toxicity. Nemvaleukin selectively binds to the intermediate-affinity IL-2 receptor, preferentially activating and expanding antitumor NK cells and CD8+ T-cells, with minimal expansion of immunosuppressive Tregs.18,19 The first-in-human ARTISTRY-1 study demonstrated antitumor activity of nemvaleukin as monotherapy and in combination with pembrolizumab at 6 µg/kg IV once daily for 5 consecutive days (QD × 5) in a 21-day cycle in heavily pretreated adults with advanced solid tumors. Nemvaleukin had a manageable safety profile, allowing outpatient administration, unlike high-dose rhIL-2.20

The present open-label ARTISTRY-3 Cohort 2 study evaluated a less frequent, and, hence, more convenient IV dosing regimen of nemvaleukin that would demonstrate pharmacodynamic effects and safety similar to those achieved with the QD × 5 regimen in advanced solid tumors. Of 52 patients who received nemvaleukin, most had stage IV disease (83%) and had received a median of 4 (range, 1-14) prior lines of therapy (Table 1). Nemvaleukin 30 μg/kg at days 1 and 8 of a 21-day cycle was declared the RP2D, based on a comprehensive review of safety, pharmacokinetic, and pharmacodynamic parameters across all schedules and doses.

td.bold: Patients with ≥3 prior lines of therapy, n (%)

fn.label: a
fn.p: Unknown/missing in 9 (53%)/2 (12%) patients in Schedule 1, 4 (31%)/4 (31%) patients in Schedule 2, and 14 (64%)/3 (14%) patients in Schedule 3.

fn.label: b
fn.p: Missing in 15 (88%) patients in Schedule 1, 11 (85%) patients in Schedule 2, and 21 (95%) patients in Schedule 3.

fn.label: c
fn.p: Unknown in 10 (59%) patients in Schedule 1, 10 (77%) patients in Schedule 2, and 11 (50%) patients in Schedule 3.

fn.label: d
fn.p: Unknown/missing in 11 (65%)/0 patients in Schedule 1, 10 (77%)/1 (8%) patients in Schedule 2, and 13 (59%)/0 patients in Schedule 3.

fn.label: e
fn.p: Unknown in 11 (65%) patients in Schedule 1, 11 (85%) patients in Schedule 2, and 14 (64%) patients in Schedule 3.
Abbreviations: BRCA, breast cancer gene; HR, homologous recombination; BRAF, B-Raf proto-oncogene; KIT, KIT proto-oncogene; NRAS, neuroblastoma RAS viral oncogene homolog; SD, standard deviation.

sec.p: Across the escalating doses, nemvaleukin was generally well tolerated and demonstrated a manageable safety profile in all 3 dosing schedules. Nemvaleukin exposure increased with increasing doses without evidence of nonlinearity. No DLTs were observed across the 3 dosing schedules, and all evaluated doses were declared tolerable by the safety review committee. The most common (≥20% in at least 1 schedule) treatment-related adverse events (TRAEs) were infusion-related reaction, cytokine release syndrome, fatigue, pyrexia, nausea, hypotension, and neutropenia. TRAEs reported in all 3 schedules were mostly low grade and resolved with standard management protocols. In all, 6 (12%) patients experienced grade 3 TRAEs: anemia and fatigue in Schedule 2 and neutropenia, lymphopenia, cytokine release syndrome, and decreased white blood cell count in Schedule 3. Neutropenia was the most common and only observed TRAE in Schedule 3. No patient experienced TRAEs that led to treatment discontinuation. No grade 4 or 5 TRAEs were observed. No major differences in safety profiles were observed across the 3 schedules, although sample sizes were small. Importantly, despite the use of higher nemvaleukin doses than the 6 μg/kg QD × 5 dosing in ARTISTRY-1, no new safety signals were identified in ARTISTRY-3. Grade 3 events of neutropenia were transient and were not associated with serious infection, consistent with ARTISTRY-1 findings. Notably, neutropenic events occurred less frequently in ARTISTRY-3 than in ARTISTRY-1.20 Collectively, the safety profile observed with less frequent IV dosing of nemvaleukin was consistent with its known mechanism of action and was as expected in patients with relapsed/refractory solid tumors.18–20

Pharmacodynamic analyses showed that the less frequent doses of nemvaleukin resulted in selective immune cell activation. Expansion of NK cells and CD8+ T-cells was observed with nemvaleukin across all schedules, with low-level, transient, non-dose-dependent expansion of Tregs (Figure 4A and B), confirming nemvaleukin’s design hypothesis.18,19 The expansion of NK cells and CD8+ T-cells was comparable to that achieved with QD × 5 IV dosing, with minimal impact on Tregs.21 Immune cell expansion higher than nemvaleukin’s QD × 5 dosing was observed at higher doses in Schedules 2 and 3 (≥25 μg/kg) but not in Schedule 1, suggesting 1 dose of nemvaleukin per cycle was insufficient for comparable immune cell activation. Maximum expansion in NK cells and CD8+ T-cells from baseline was observed in the 30 μg/kg group in Schedule 2 and in the 35 μg/kg group in Schedule 3. The selected RP2D regimen of 1 infusion each on days 1 and 8 offers a 7-day window for recovery between doses and was more convenient than the day 1, day 4 schedule.

Deep immune profiling through comparison of on-treatment and pre-treatment biopsies demonstrated favorable selective immune activation with less frequent IV nemvaleukin dosing regimens. Increased on-treatment density of NK cell and CD8+ T-cell subsets was observed, and ratios of effector NK cells and CD8+ T-cells to Tregs were favorable for the less frequent dosing regimen, suggesting a shift toward a proinflammatory immune milieu dominated by cytolytic effector NK cells and CD8+ T-cells. This expansion was seen in biopsies from patients with mucosal melanoma and those with ovarian cancer, tumor types known to be poorly responsive to immunotherapy.22–24 The observations in the TME are complementary to those observed in the periphery, providing proof of principle for preferential expansion of immunostimulatory effector cells over immunosuppressive Tregs. While some patients showed an increase in Tregs alongside effector cells, this pattern was inconsistent and could be the indirect effect of bystander immune activation.21 Importantly, the ratio of effector cells to Tregs increased on treatment, even with the increase in absolute density of Tregs. These results indicate that nemvaleukin may have the potential to recruit cytolytic effectors to poorly immunogenic tumor sites, supporting its use in combination immunotherapies.25,26

As this was a dose escalation study that utilized multiple doses and dosing schedules, in a heavily pretreated population with heterogeneous advanced tumor types, only preliminary antitumor activity of less frequent IV nemvaleukin was observed. Although no objective responses were observed, 16 (31%) patients achieved stable disease and 6 (12%) had stable disease for ≥3 months (Figure 2A). Up to 27% reduction in target lesion was observed across all patients, with maximum reduction in tumor size of at least 20% observed in 5 patients from Schedules 2 and 3 (Figure 2B). One patient maintained reduction for up to 35 weeks.

The ARTISTRY-3 study had a few limitations. Owing to the phase I/II study design, the sample sizes for the dosing groups in each schedule were small, and the RP2D analysis was based on 3-5 patients enrolled in each schedule. The study population was heterogeneous for tumor types and heavily pretreated for advanced disease, making it difficult to detect any benefit of nemvaleukin on efficacy, particularly by tumor type. TME analyses evaluated the pharmacodynamic effects of nemvaleukin among patients from different schedules and tumors biopsied at different times after nemvaleukin treatment. Additional studies are needed to evaluate nemvaleukin in a homogeneous tumor type with defined prior lines of treatment.

In conclusion, ARTISTRY-3 identified a less frequent dosing schedule of nemvaleukin that was patient- and provider-friendly compared with the more frequent QD × 5 regimen. The safety profile was manageable and consistent with QD × 5 dosing, while the pharmacodynamic profile aligned with nemvaleukin’s mechanism of action. Clinical activity of this less frequent IV dosing was limited, as this was a heterogeneous and heavily pre-treated population. Nevertheless, immune activation was observed in the TME upon nemvaleukin treatment, consistent with its peripheral immune dynamics and proposed mechanism of action. The observed safety profile, coupled with immune cell activation in both TME and periphery, supports further clinical investigation of less frequent nemvaleukin dosing in larger clinical trials with a homogenous patient population.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: This study was supported by Mural Oncology, Inc. We thank the patients and their families who made this study possible, and the investigators and the clinical study teams for study support. Professional medical writing and editorial assistance were provided by Mandakini Singh, PhD, of Parexel International and funded by Mural Oncology, Inc. Results reported in this manuscript were previously presented in part at the American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) congress (May 31-June 4, 2024) as a poster (Abstract # 2587).

sec.title: Author contributions
sec.p: Sarina A. Piha-Paul (Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing Original Draft, Writing Review Editing), Justin A. Call (Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing Original Draft, Writing Review Editing), Alexander I. Spira (Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing Original Draft, Writing Review Editing), Jorge Bartolomé (Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing Original Draft, Writing Review Editing), Maria de Miguel (Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing Original Draft, Writing Review Editing), Xiwei Chen (Data Curation, Formal Analysis, Validation, Writing Original Draft, Writing Review Editing), Sarah S. Donatelli (Data Curation, Formal Analysis, Validation, Writing Original Draft, Writing Review Editing), and Nehal J. Lakhani (Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing Original Draft, Writing Review Editing)

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: The study was sponsored by Mural Oncology; the sponsor, in collaboration with the investigators, participated in the study design, data collection, data analysis, data interpretation, and writing of this report.

sec.title: Conflicts of interest
sec.p: S.A.P-P declares research funding to the institution from ABM Therapeutics, Alkermes, Aminex Therapeutics, Axcynsis Therapeutics Pte. Ltd., BioMarin Pharmaceutical, Boehringer Ingelheim, Chugai Pharmaceutical, Cullinan Oncology, Cyclacel Pharmaceuticals, Day One Biopharmaceuticals Inc, Daiichi Sankyo, ENB Therapeutics, Epigenetix, Genmab A/S, Gilead Sciences, Hengrui Pharmaceuticals, Immunomedics, Immunity Bio, Inc, Immunome, Inc., Incyte Corp., Innovent Biologics, iTeos Belgium SA, Jazz Pharmaceuticals, Johnson & Johnson, Loxo Oncology, Merck Sharp and Dohme Corp., Mitsubishi Tanabe Pharma America (MTPA) Inc., Nectin Therapeutics, Nested Therapeutics, Inc., NRG Oncology, Nurix, OncoNano Medicine, Pyxis Oncology, Pieris Pharmaceuticals, Pfizer, Phanes Therapeutics, Puma Biotechnology, Purinomia Biotech, Replimune, Roche/Blueprint, Solve Therapeutics, Strand Therapeutics, Tallac Therapeutics, Toragen Therapeutics, TransThera Bio, Xencor, NCI/NIH- P30CA016672—Core Grant (CCSG Shared Resources), CPRIT Grant: Cancer Prevention Research Institute of Texas (CPRIT) Precision Oncology Decision Support Core (RP150535) and Clinical and Translational Science Award Grant (CTSA): 1UM1TR0045906. She has also worked as a consultant for Mitsubishi Tanabe Pharma America (MTPA) Inc.. J.A.C. declares no competing interests. A.I.S. declares a leadership role with NEXT Oncology Virginia (institution); honoraria fee from AbbVie, Amgen, Astellas Pharma, AstraZeneca/MedImmune, Bayer, Bristol Myers Squibb, CytomX Therapeutics, Janssen Oncology, Merck, Novartis, Prelude Therapeutics, and Takeda; consulting fee and ad boards from Amgen, ArriVent Biopharma, Black Diamond Therapeutics, CRISPR Therapeutics, Daiichi Sankyo/AstraZeneca, Gritstone Bio, Gritstone Oncology, GSK, Incyte, Janssen Research & Development, Jazz Pharmaceuticals, Lilly, Mersana, Mirati Therapeutics, Novartis, Regeneron, Sanofi, Takeda, and Synthekine; research funding from LAM Therapeutics; funding to the institution from AbbVie, ADCT, Alkermes, Amgen, ArriVent Biopharma, AstraZeneca, Astellas Pharma, Astex Pharmaceuticals, Black Diamond Therapeutics, Blueprint Medicines, BluPrint Oncology, Boehringer Ingelheim, Bristol Myers Squibb, CytomX Therapeutics, Daiichi Sankyo, Gritstone, Ignyta, Incyte, Janssen Oncology, Loxo, Macrogenics, Medikine, MedImmune, Merck, Mersana, Nalo Therapeutics, Novartis, Plexxikon, Prelude Therapeutics, Regeneron, Revolution Medicines, Roche, Rubius, Scorpion Therapeutics, Synthekine, and Takeda. J.B. declares no competing interests. M.M. declares grants from AbbVie, Achilles, Array, Basilea, Bayer, BioNTech, Catalym, Cytomex, Faron, Genentech, Genmab, Janssen, Menarini, MSD, Nektar, Novartis, Pharmamar, Regeneron, Repare, and Zenith; consulting fee and ad boards from Novartis, Repare, and Syneoshealth. X.C. and S.S.D. declare employment and stock options in Mural Oncology. N.J.L. declares nonfinancial support from Mural Oncology during the conduct of the study; other support outside of submitted work from Alexion, Alpine, ALX Therapeutics, Apexian, Asana, Ascentage, BeiGene, Cerulean, Constellation Pharma/Morphosys, CytomX, Formation Biologics (Forbius), Forty-Seven, Incyte, InhibRx, Innovent Biologics, Jounce, Livzon, Merck, Northern Biologics, Pfizer, Regeneron, Sapience Therapeutics, and Symphogen; personal fees from Alpine, Arcus Bio, Artios, Astellas, Bristol Myers Squibb, Celgene, Gilead, GSK, Helsinn, Ikena, Incyte, Innovent Biologics, Janssen, KSQ Therapeutics/Roche, Loxo/Lilly, Macrogenics, Mersana, Odonate, Quanta Therapeutics, Repare Therapeutics, Revolution Medicines, Samumed, Sapience, Seagen/Pfizer, Servier, Shattuck Labs, SK Life Sciences, Tizona, and Volastra.

sec.title: Data availability
sec.p: Data will be made available upon reasonable request. The data sets used and/or analyzed in the current study will be available after review of the request and approval from the sponsor and co-authors.
